title: Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries
summary: How to collect rainwater for non-drinking use first, while keeping contamination risks visible.
content: Rainwater can reduce pressure on stored water, but roofs, gutters, dust, bird droppings, smoke residue, and dirty containers can contaminate it. Treat unknown rainwater as non-drinking unless a safe treatment plan and local guidance support drinking use. Use it for flushing, cleaning, or plants before considering any higher-risk use. Keep first runoff away from storage when possible. Label containers clearly so nobody mistakes untreated water for drinking water.
materials: Clean storage space. Context: “Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries”.
steps: Divert the first dirty runoff when possible.
Cover storage to stop insects and debris.
Label as untreated unless treated and confirmed safe.
warnings: Do not collect from roofs contaminated by chemicals, ash, dead animals, or sewage.
Do not assume rainwater is automatically safe to drink.
common_mistakes: Treating Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries as finished after the first attempt instead of writing the next review time.
Moving people or supplies for Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries without checking vulnerable people, exits, and safe limits first.
Mixing clean and unsafe items during Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries because labels, dates, or responsible names were not written down.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.
The routine ignores water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries.
short_term: For the first hours of Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Rainwater capture hygiene boundaries to the real environment: check water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
